artifacts.content:
Hi Karen,

Just circling back after yesterday’s chat about Michael Tran and the Mascot incident, because it’s still bothering me how exposed we were.

On 14 March 2024 when Michael slipped off that temporary gantry at the DHL Express warehouse in Mascot, he was literally just supervising the conveyor upgrade and giving the riggers direction. Yet once he’d been evacuated and kept overnight at Prince of Wales Hospital in Randwick, we found out the current travel policy was only going to pick up part of it because of the way the manual work exclusion is worded. From where I sit, that’s completely out of step with how our commissioning work actually happens.

It’s the same story as Rotterdam in January – I ended up on the 2.5‑metre mezzanine at the DB Schenker logistics hub with Aaron Patel, physically re‑aligning a misfed conveyor sensor because the subcontractor walked off site mid‑task. Our internal incident note even says if either of us had gone over the edge we might have been knocked back on a claim because it looked like “installation” rather than “supervision”. That’s not a one‑off anymore, it’s just how these projects run.

Given we’ve now got grads and project coordinators flying all over the place as well, I really think we need to treat incidental hands‑on work as part of the normal risk profile, not an exception. Happy to sit down with you and Luke to go through the wording and pull examples from recent trips so we can brief Harbourview properly.

Thanks,
Daniel

Daniel Mark Henson  |  Engineering Director
Southern Cross Engineering Pty Ltd
M: +61 414 298 673

metadata.subject: Michael Tran Mascot incident and manual work exposure

artifacts.content:
Hey Em,

I’m back at the hotel in Chicago and finally have a minute. It’s been a pretty rough week. David McMillan’s still in ICU at Northwestern Memorial – today is day 7 of what they say will be at least 9 days in intensive care for the pneumonia. The doctors are cautiously positive, but the costs over here are insane. The hospital bill alone is already tracking at just over USD 310k, and when the air ambulance quote to get him back to Sydney came in, I realised how close we are to hitting that AUD 5m cap on the current travel policy.

I keep thinking about my own stuff as well – that arrhythmia episode I had in Singapore in November 2024, and the follow‑up testing Dr Andrew Patel has booked at Royal North Shore for 15–28 March 2025. Karen Willis has already pulled me off any long‑haul runs in that window, just domestic hops under three hours, which I’m actually grateful for.

On top of that we’re still chipping away at the $18,000 ATO debt from March. It’s part of why I’ve been saying yes to those 9–10 day trips to Auckland and Manila – the on‑site day rates help, but weeks like this make me wonder how sustainable it all is.

Kids sound good on FaceTime, but Sophie looked wiped after netball. Can you please remind her I’ll be back in time for her CBT session at Northern Beaches next fortnight? I don’t want her thinking I’ve forgotten.

I’ll call again your evening. Try to get some sleep.

Love,
D

metadata.subject: David in ICU and thinking about our own risk

artifacts.content:
Hi Liv,

Just got back from Hamburg and wanted to give you the full story before Mum hears a garbled version.

We sent our new grad, Hannah Lewis, over for a 6‑day visit to the Siemens plant in Hamburg in July – her first proper overseas trip. She was staying at the Courtyard by Marriott Hamburg City. On night two, someone got into her room while she was at dinner and nicked her laptop and passport. She was pretty shaken.

The really galling bit is that when we went to lodge the claim, it turned out she technically wasn’t an “insured person” because her employment contract hadn’t finished processing in MYOB before she flew. Completely ridiculous – she was travelling for us, at our request, but the policy definition hadn’t caught up with how fast we’re hiring.

It’s made me rethink how we’re handling the grads generally. Between Hannah in Hamburg, Michael Tran’s fall from that temporary gantry at the DHL Express warehouse in Mascot back in March, and my own hands‑on episode with Aaron at the DB Schenker logistics hub mezzanine in Rotterdam in January, the old idea that engineers just “supervise” from the sidelines is fantasy.

On the home front, I’m still trying to keep the overseas trips to about 7 days on average since Sophie’s anxiety diagnosis at the Northern Beaches Child & Adolescent Mental Health Service in May 2023. Her fortnightly CBT sessions in Balgowlah seem to help, but she gets wobbly if I’m away over two weekends in a row.

Anyway, enough shop talk. How are the kids? We should line up a Newcastle weekend once Mum’s finished this round of rehab.

Dan

metadata.subject: Hannah’s Hamburg saga and juggling travel

artifacts.content:
Mate,

Finally back from Spain and my ankle’s the size of a small melon, so thought I’d fill you in before we catch up for a beer.

The Maersk logistics hub meetings in Barcelona went well enough, but I decided to stay on for an extra 7 days in April so Emily could fly over and we could have a breather. We based ourselves partly in the city and partly down in Sitges with Mum and Dad’s old friends, the Donovans. Classic Dan move: on day 6 of “relaxing”, I rolled my ankle on a dodgy step and ended up at Hospital Clínic de Barcelona for X‑rays and strapping.

The kicker was discovering our corporate travel policy only recognises 5 days of private leisure extension after a work trip. Because I’d tagged on 7 days, the last couple weren’t covered and I ended up about AUD 1,350 out of pocket. Not the end of the world, but annoying enough that I’m pushing to get the business/leisure cover extended to 10 days.

I’m also using it as ammo with Ben after that $42k Jakarta write‑off in August 2024 – seven staff with non‑refundable flights and hotels when the supplier defaulted. He wants us to be super conservative on conferences now (basically just National Manufacturing Week in Melbourne and the Singapore symposium in May 2025), but I’m arguing that if we’re going to send people long‑haul, we should at least recognise reality about how they travel.

Anyway, ankle is fine, Emily got her Sitges sunsets, and the kids mostly just wanted more Barça merch. Let’s lock in that Manly game once I can walk without limping.

Dan

metadata.subject: Barcelona, busted ankle and travel cover

artifacts.content:
Personal running log / health + travel

I keep coming back to how thin the margins are between work, family, and my own body giving out.

Houston was the eye‑opener: 4½‑week stint from 10 April to 12 May 2023 for the compressor failure investigation after the subcontractor fatality at the Baytown site. That’s still the longest I’ve been away – about 35 days – and I came home wrecked. Now we’re talking about similar 4–5 week runs in places like Port Moresby (covering Mark Ellison if he goes back on stress leave after the Lae refinery control room breakdown) or a Jurong retrofit in Singapore while Dad does prostate cancer radiotherapy at Mount Elizabeth. I need to be honest about what that does to me.

Then there was Singapore in November 2024, when I had that minor arrhythmia episode in the hotel after a 16‑hour day. Dr Andrew Patel at Royal North Shore has me booked in for follow‑up cardiac testing 15–28 March 2025 and Karen’s capped me at domestic flights under three hours in that window. Probably overdue.

Layer on Sophie’s anxiety (Northern Beaches CAMHS, May 2023, fortnightly CBT in Balgowlah) and Emily starting chemo at Chris O’Brien Lifehouse on 10 Feb 2025 for six months, and the idea of another 35‑day overseas trip feels irresponsible.

Action points:
– Keep most trips to 7–10 days; 35‑day maximum only if absolutely critical.
– Say no to back‑to‑back long‑hauls while Emily’s in treatment.
– Keep running around North Harbour 3x/week.
– Block out kids’ key dates before agreeing to anything new.

Event: Newcastle – client safety audit + visit Mum in rehab
Date: 12–14 March 2025
Location: Newcastle – client site / John Hunter Hospital
Attendees: Daniel Henson; client safety auditor; Margaret Henson (visit)

Notes:
– Drive up early 12 March from Balgowlah. Check in to Ibis Budget Newcastle (2 nights).
– Day 1: Meet with client’s safety auditor re machinery fault on Line 3 press that injured their operator. Go through our commissioning records and maintenance logs.
– Evenings: Visit Mum in the rehab ward at John Hunter Hospital after her minor stroke. Coordinate with Olivia and Dad on home‑care roster once she’s discharged back to Balgowlah.

Reminders:
– Keep local trips to 1–3 days where possible – ties in with decision after Sophie’s anxiety diagnosis at Northern Beaches Child & Adolescent Mental Health Service (May 2023) to be home before her fortnightly CBT sessions.
– Don’t commit to any overseas travel overlapping 15–28 March 2025 – blocked out for follow‑up cardiac testing with Dr Andrew Patel at Royal North Shore Hospital after the arrhythmia episode in Singapore (Nov 2024). HR (Karen Willis) has already limited me to domestic flights under 3 hours in that period.
– Check in with Emily about her chemo schedule at Chris O’Brien Lifehouse (started 10 Feb 2025, ~6 months) before accepting any extra site visits.

Bring: laptop, incident file, Mum’s discharge summary, running shoes (try to get a jog in along the foreshore one morning).

metadata.title: Newcastle – safety audit & Mum rehab visits

artifacts.content:
Hi Ben,

Ahead of the board risk catch‑up I wanted to give you some colour on why I’m pushing so hard on the travel cover and approvals.

On the medical side, David McMillan’s 9‑day ICU stay at Northwestern Memorial in Chicago last November came in at just over USD 310,000 before we even looked at the air ambulance quote back to Sydney. That brushed right up against our current AUD 5m aggregate overseas medical/evac cap. If we have a second serious incident in the same policy year – even something shorter – we’re exposed. I’d be a lot more comfortable at AUD 7.5m.

On the injury side, Michael Tran’s slip from the temporary gantry at the DHL Express warehouse in Mascot on 14 March 2024 and my own hands‑on work with Aaron on the 2.5‑metre mezzanine at DB Schenker Rotterdam in January both highlight how fuzzy the line is between “supervision” and “manual work” in real life. Our current wording still assumes engineers stay on the ground.

Then there are the edge cases: Hannah Lewis in Hamburg in July 2024, travelling to the Siemens plant for 6 days before her contract fully hit MYOB, finding out after the theft at the Courtyard by Marriott Hamburg City that she technically wasn’t an insured person. Or my own Barcelona trip in April 2024 where I stayed on an extra 7 days after the Maersk meetings, sprained my ankle in Sitges, and ended up AUD 1,350 out of pocket at Hospital Clínic because the policy only recognises 5 days of leisure extension.

Layer that over the $42k Jakarta write‑off in August 2024 (7 staff, non‑refundable flights and hotels after the supplier default) and it’s obvious we need both tighter approval discipline and a policy that actually matches how we travel.

Happy to walk through this in more detail on Monday.

Dan

metadata.subject: Travel risk, cover gaps and conference approvals
